texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chemical separation accounts for about a half of the energy used in industrial processes and about 10 to 15% of the national energy consumption[1]. In order to meet the growing energy demand, it is crucial to explore new energy-saving technologies to reduce energy consumed in separation processes. As a new approach of separation technology, researchers predict that membrane-based separation can save up to 90% of energy compared to thermal distillation for separating mixtures[1,2]. In this context, gas membrane separation technology has demonstrated a wide range of applications in the fields of carbon capture[3,4], natural gas purification[5] and air separation[6]. As an attractive class of gas membrane materials, polyimides have been studied and used widely since their commercialization in 1980 s[7]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymers  of  intrinsic  microporosity  (PIMs)  have  seen  a  growing  role  in  gas  separation  membranes.  Further exploitation  of  gas  separation  performance  of  PIM  membranes  and  overcoming  their  persistent  challenge of plasticization are urgently demanded. To address these issues, exemplified PIM-1 is synthesized and hydrolyzed to form carboxylated PIM-COOH polymers, followed by esterification using propylene glycol to prepare monoesterified PIM, which is finally thermally ester-crosslinked at various temperatures and time. The 300 ◦ C-8 h treated membranes exhibit a CO2 permeability of 7421 Barrer, with a CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivity of 11.5 and 19.2, respectively, considerably exceeding the 2008 Robeson upper bounds. Furthermore, the crosslinked membranes  demonstrate  significantly  enhanced  CO2  plasticization  resistance  up  to  42  bar,  compared  with uncrosslinked ones. It is the first time of reporting ester-crosslinking of carboxylated PIM-1 with both higher gas permeability and selectivity than PIM-1, using a much milder crosslinking temperature than other techniques, such  as  decarboxylated  crosslinking.  The  design  principle  of  this  work  paves  the  way  for  preparing  highperformance and plasticization-resistant PIM membranes with promises for energy-efficient gas separations.

texts.orig: In  recent  years,  polymers  of  intrinsic  microporosity  (PIMs)  have received wide attention in gas separation membranes due to their ultrahigh gas permeability[11,12]. In 2004, PIM-1 was synthesized as a new microporous polymer with a high BET area up to 800 m 2 /g and pore size below 2 nm[13]. Later, PIM-1 was applied to fabricate gas separation membranes and achieved a CO2  permeability as high as 5000 Barrer, exceeding the 1991 Robeson upper bond[14]. Since then, a wide array of PIM materials was developed and employed in the preparation of gas separation membranes.

texts.orig: In order to further enhance the gas separation performance of PIM membranes, two major approaches have been identified and explored extensively [12,15]. texts.orig: conditions. Despite the decrease of gas permeability for CO2, H2, N2 and other gases, the addition of carboxylic groups results in an increase of selectivity of CO2/CH4, CO2/N2  and H2/N2  for PIM-COOH, compared with  PIM-1.  Jeon  et  al.[20]  prepared  up  to  92%  carboxylated  PIMCOOH via  an  extended  alkali  hydrolysis  process.  While  the  CO2/N2 selectivity of the PIM-COOH membrane is improved to 53.6, the CO2 permeability decreases to 96.4 Barrer, a reduction of 97.5% compared with PIM-1 (Pco2 = 3934 Barrer). Furthermore, Weng et al.[19] and Rodriguez  et  al.[18]  obtained  carboxylated  PIM-COOH  membranes through an acidic hydrolysis process in the presence of acetic acid. Besides post-carboxylic modification of nitrile groups in PIM-1, chemical modification processes such as amine[21], amidoxime[22,23], tetrazole [24],  and  amide[25]  have  been  reported  for  functionalizing  PIM-1 membranes.  Nevertheless, there still exist permeability-selectivity trade-offs for PIM-1 membranes by using the chemical modification of nitrile groups in PIM-1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The other approach to improve performance of PIM membranes aims to design and prepare new  molecular structures[11]. In 2019, Comesa ˜ na-G ´ andara  et  al.[8]  synthesized  a  series  of  PIMs  based  on benzotriptycene structural units, which enabled the PIM membranes to obtain  ultra-high  gas  permeability.  Accordingly,  they  redefined  the upper  bounds  for  CO2/CH4 and  CO2/N2  separations  based  on  PIM membranes with benzotriptycene units. Other PIMs, such as PIM-TMNTrip, PIM-TMN-SBI[26], PIM-EA-TB and PIMSBI-TB[27] were synthesized  for  gas  separation  membranes and demonstrated appealing gas permeability. Notwithstanding the success of creating new PIM structures, physical aging and plasticization remain persistent challenges for PIM membranes, which significantly limit the practical application of PIMs for efficient gas separations. In particular, induced by a high CO2 partial  feed  pressure,  plasticization  decreases  the  gas  selectivity  and limits the membrane separation efficiency[10,28]. An indicator of the degree of plasticization is the plasticizing pressure, corresponding to the pressure of inflection point when the fugacity-corrected permeability starts to increase with rising feed pressure[29].

In 2019, Comesa ˜ na-G ´ andara  et  al.[8]  synthesized  a  series  of  PIMs  based  on benzotriptycene structural units, which enabled the PIM membranes to obtain  ultra-high  gas  permeability.  Accordingly,  they  redefined  the upper  bounds  for  CO2/CH4 and  CO2/N2  separations  based  on  PIM membranes with benzotriptycene units. Other PIMs, such as PIM-TMNTrip, PIM-TMN-SBI[26], PIM-EA-TB and PIMSBI-TB[27] were synthesized  for  gas  separation  membranes and demonstrated appealing gas permeability. Notwithstanding the success of creating new PIM structures, physical aging and plasticization remain persistent challenges for PIM membranes, which significantly limit the practical application of PIMs for efficient gas separations. In particular, induced by a high CO2 partial  feed  pressure,  plasticization  decreases  the  gas  selectivity  and limits the membrane separation efficiency[10,28]. An indicator of the degree of plasticization is the plasticizing pressure, corresponding to the pressure of inflection point when the fugacity-corrected permeability starts to increase with rising feed pressure[29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermal crosslinking strategy has been discovered as an facile and effective solution to tackle the plasticization of PIM membranes [10,30]. Du et al.[31] investigated the thermal decarboxylation of crosslinked PIM-COOH membranes at 375 ◦ C. The CO2  permeability of the crosslinked  membrane  remains  stable  with  a  feed  pressure  up  to  55  bar, indicating the absence of CO2 plasticization. However, the CO2 permeability  of  crosslinked  membranes drops to 1291 Barrer, compared to PIM-1 membranes (5093 Barrer). Song et al.[32] and Chen et al.[33] researched thermally crosslinked neat and blended PIM membranes in the presence of ppm levels of oxygen atmosphere. It was found that the trace  oxygen  boosts  the  selectivity  of  PIM  membranes  and  endows crosslinked membranes with anti-aging and anti-plasticizing properties [33]. Li et al.[34] thermally treated PIM-1 for a prolonged period of time to  form  a  triazine  ring  structure  based  on  aromatic  cyano  groups. Thermally crosslinked PIM-1 at 300 ◦ C for 2 days obtained ideal CO2/ CH4  and CO2/N2  selectivity of 54.8 and 41.7 respectively, while possessing  CO2  permeability  up  to  4000  Barrer.  Despite  those  attempts, most  of  the  crosslinked  PIM  membranes  methods  suffer  from  high crosslinking temperature, extended crosslinking time and drastic loss of gas  permeability.  In  previous  work[7,35 -37],  ester-crosslinking  has been proposed as an effective approach to enhance aging- resistance and plasticization-resistance on 6FDA-based polyimide membranes. Particularly,  excellent  crosslinked  structures  achieved  at  crosslinking  temperatures as low as 200 ◦ C and crosslinking time shorter than 2 h have a large potential for application in natural gas separation. To the best of our knowledge, the ester crosslinking method has not been reported to functionalize PIM-1 and form crosslinked PIM-1 membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  address  the  CO2 plasticization  PIM  membranes,  herein,  we investigate  the  ester-crosslinking  approach  to  create  a  crosslinked structure of PIM-COOH membranes. Firstly, PIM-1 is synthesized and hydrolyzed in acidic conditions to prepare PIM-COOH. As an excellent candidate  of  crosslinking  agents,  propylene  glycol  was  judiciously selected  to  be  grafted  into  PIM-COOH  to  form  monoesterified  PIM. Although propylene glycol was used for the  esterification  of  flexible polyimides [7], but to our best knowledge, it has not been reported in esterifying the semi-rigid carboxylated PIM-1 polymer. Finally, monoesterified  PIM  was  thermally  treated  to  obtain  ester  crosslinked  PIM membranes. It is worth noting that thermal treatment temperature and time  have  critical  influence  on  the  performance  of  crosslinked  PIM membranes.  Hence,  our  research  systematically  explores  effects  of thermal treatment time and temperature on molecular structure and gas performance  of  membranes.  It  is  the  first  time  of  reporting  estercrosslinking  of  carboxylated  PIMs.  Compared  with  PIM-1,  the  estercrosslinked  PIM  demonstrates  both  enhanced  gas  permeability  and selectivity, using a crosslinking temperature as low as 200 ◦ C, milder than the reported decarboxylated crosslinking of PIM-1 (e.g. 375 ◦ C) [31]. Furthermore, this study creates ester crosslinked structures of PIM membranes  with  enhanced  CO2  plasticization  resistance,  having  potentials for practical applications in aggressive gas separations under high CO2 feed pressures.

texts.orig: Materials. Toluene, chloroform, concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, greater  than  98%),  acetic  acid  (CH3COOH),  methanol,  N-methylpyrrolidone and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Group and used as received. Anhydrous potassium carbonate (K2CO3),  p-toluenesulfonic  acid  and  propylene  glycol  were  obtained from  Aladdin.  5,  5 ′ 6,  6 ′ -Tetrahydroxy-3,  3 ′ ,  3,  3 ′ -tetramethyl  spirobisinadane (TTSBI, Alfa Aesar) recrystallized in methanol before use. 2, 3, 5, 6-tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile (TFTPN, Macklin) was purified by vacuum sublimation at 150 ◦ C before use.
texts.text: Materials. Toluene, chloroform, concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, greater  than  98%),  acetic  acid  (CH3COOH),  methanol,  N-methylpyrrolidone and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Group and used as received. Anhydrous potassium carbonate (K2CO3),  p-toluenesulfonic  acid  and  propylene  glycol  were  obtained from  Aladdin.  5,  5 ′ 6,  6 ′ -Tetrahydroxy-3,  3 ′ ,  3,  3 ′ -tetramethyl  spirobisinadane (TTSBI, Alfa Aesar) recrystallized in methanol before use. 2, 3, 5, 6-tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile (TFTPN, Macklin) was purified by vacuum sublimation at 150 ◦ C before use.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthesis of PIM-1 Polymers. PIM-1 was synthesized according to previous  references[38,39].  In  Fig.  S1,  a  three-neck  flask  was  firstly added with 30 mmol (2.0 g) TFTPN, 30 mmol (3.4 g) TTSBI and 60 mmol (4.2 g) anhydrous K2CO3, followed by 20 ml anhydrous DMAC and 10 ml toluene, and the mixture was mechanically stirred at room temperature for about 20 min. Then the mixture was heated to 155 ◦ C and soaked for 60 min under N2 atmosphere. After the reaction, the mixture was reprecipitated and washed with methanol. Finally, the light yellow solid was collected through filtration and dried under vacuum at 120 ◦ C for 24 h. The molecular weight of the pristine PIM-1 was determined via gel permeation chromatography (using the chloroform as solvent), showing a molecular weight of MW = 120000 Da and a polydispersity of 2.08.
texts.text: Synthesis of PIM-1 Polymers. PIM-1 was synthesized according to previous  references[38,39].  In  Fig.  S1,  a  three-neck  flask  was  firstly added with 30 mmol (2.0 g) TFTPN, 30 mmol (3.4 g) TTSBI and 60 mmol (4.2 g) anhydrous K2CO3, followed by 20 ml anhydrous DMAC and 10 ml toluene, and the mixture was mechanically stirred at room temperature for about 20 min. Then the mixture was heated to 155 ◦ C and soaked for 60 min under N2 atmosphere. After the reaction, the mixture was reprecipitated and washed with methanol. Finally, the light yellow solid was collected through filtration and dried under vacuum at 120 ◦ C for 24 h. The molecular weight of the pristine PIM-1 was determined via gel permeation chromatography (using the chloroform as solvent), showing a molecular weight of MW = 120000 Da and a polydispersity of 2.08.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthesis  of  PIM-COOH  Polymers. PIM-COOH  was  synthesized using the previously reported scheme by Weng et al[19]. As shown in Fig. S2, 2.0 g PIM-1 was added to 60 ml 50 wt% H2SO4  solution, followed by 20 ml CH3COOH, and the reaction was carried out at 105 ◦ C for 48 h under reflux condensation. After reaction, the mixture was washed with water and methanol for 3 times, and dried under vacuum at 80 ◦ C for 24 h to obtain dark yellow powders.
texts.text: Synthesis  of  PIM-COOH  Polymers. PIM-COOH  was  synthesized using the previously reported scheme by Weng et al[19]. As shown in Fig. S2, 2.0 g PIM-1 was added to 60 ml 50 wt% H2SO4  solution, followed by 20 ml CH3COOH, and the reaction was carried out at 105 ◦ C for 48 h under reflux condensation. After reaction, the mixture was washed with water and methanol for 3 times, and dried under vacuum at 80 ◦ C for 24 h to obtain dark yellow powders.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthesis  of  monoesterified  PIM  Polymers. The  synthesis  of monoesterified PIM (M PIM) were performed based on the reference reported by Ma et al.[7] with modified procedures as shown in Fig. S3. In detail, 1.0 g PIM-COOH and 40 ml of anhydrous NMP were added in a three-neck flask and then heated to 80 ◦ C for dissolving the polymer under N2  atmosphere. Further, the mixture was heated to 105 ◦ C and added 10 ml anhydrous toluene and 0.276 g p-toluenesulfonic acid as catalyst.  Then,  13.1  g  propylene  glycol  as  crosslinkers  was  added dropwise to the solution. Finally, the mixture was kept at 105 ◦ C for 20 h. After the reaction, the mixture was washed three times in methanol and dried under vacuum at 80 ◦ C.
texts.text: Synthesis  of  monoesterified  PIM  Polymers. The  synthesis  of monoesterified PIM (M PIM) were performed based on the reference reported by Ma et al.[7] with modified procedures as shown in Fig. S3. In detail, 1.0 g PIM-COOH and 40 ml of anhydrous NMP were added in a three-neck flask and then heated to 80 ◦ C for dissolving the polymer under N2  atmosphere. Further, the mixture was heated to 105 ◦ C and added 10 ml anhydrous toluene and 0.276 g p-toluenesulfonic acid as catalyst.  Then,  13.1  g  propylene  glycol  as  crosslinkers  was  added dropwise to the solution. Finally, the mixture was kept at 105 ◦ C for 20 h. After the reaction, the mixture was washed three times in methanol and dried under vacuum at 80 ◦ C.

texts.orig: FT-IR  spectra  provide  evidence  for  the  introduction  of  new  functional groups in the post-modification of PIM-1. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption peak at 2250 cm 1 corresponds to the nitrile groups, which is the  main  functional  group  of  PIM-1[13].  The  presence  of  the  nitrile absorption peaks in PIM-COOH and monoesterified PIM indicates that the conversion of -CN to -COOH is partially completed, consistent with results reported in reference [18,19]. Peaks in the range of 2750 -3100 cm 1 represent the aliphatic and aromatic C -H stretching vibrations of samples. Different from PIM-1, the absorption peaks at 3250 -3600 cm 1 correspond to -OH from carboxylic acid groups for PIM-COOH[18,20]. In the case of monoesterified M PIM, those peaks refer to -OH groups of ester  moieties  during  the  mono-esterification  process.  As  the  characteristic peak of carbonyl groups (C = O) is located at 1750 cm 1 , the evolution of C = O peaks gives further evidence of the hydrolysis and mono-esterification process. As expected, C = O peaks do not appear for PIM-1, which, however, emerged for PIM-COOH because of the nitrile group in PIM-1 was transformed to carboxyl group gradually[17]. C = O peaks  remain  in  the  FT-IR  spectrum  of  M PIM  since  the  monoesterification  process  does  not  impact  the  carbonyl  groups  of  PIMCOOH essentially. 1 H  NMR  results  of  PIM-1,  PIM-COOH  and  monoesterified PIM further support the formation of carboxylic groups and ester  groups  in  PIM-COOH and  M PIM, respectively  as  displayed  in Fig. 2 b. Apparently, the hydrogens from TTSBI groups in PIM-1, PIMCOOH  and  monoesterified  PIM  maintain  the  same  chemical  shifts. However, different from PIM-1, a new peak at 14 ppm is found in PIMCOOH,  attributed  to  hydroxyl  hydrogen  in  carboxylic  moieties.  The conversion of nitrile to carboxyl groups was estimated to be 43.5% based on 1 H NMR results, which agrees with the partial hydrolysis of PIM-1 as found  in  FT-IR  characterizations.  Moreover,  the  appearance  of  new peaks at 3.7 ppm, 4.4 ppm and 4.9 ppm for M PIM indicate the successful synthesis of monoesterified PIM due to the presence of hydroxyl groups from the crosslinking agent (i.e. propylene glycol).

texts.text: 3.2. FT-IR and dissolution test of PIM crosslinked membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to study effects of crosslinking on chemical structures of PIM membranes,  FT-IR  was  performed  on  the  samples  thermally  treated under different temperatures and time. As depicted in Fig. 3, membrane samples treated at 200 ◦ C for 2 -24 h or a temperature of 150 -350 ◦ C for 8 h demonstrate same characteristic peaks of -CH3 at 2750 -3100 cm 1 and -CN at 2250 cm 1 as untreated PIM membranes. Notably, with the increase  of  crosslinking  temperature  and  time,  the  intensities  of  hydroxyl  peaks  at  3250 -3600  cm 1 tend  to  decrease  and  gradually diminished for membrane samples examined here, suggesting the increase of degree of crosslinking since -OH groups were consumed and converted into ester groups. To seek further support for this finding, both  crosslinked  and  uncrosslinked  membranes  were  placed  in  DMF solution to explore their degrees of crosslinking (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8). Based  on  the  dissolution  test,  PIM-COOH  and  M PIM samples were soluble  in  DMF,  indicating  the  absence  of  crosslinking  within  their structures. In the case of 200 ◦ C crosslinking, a minimal 8 h thermal treatment appears to be required to achieve substantial crosslinking. When increasing the temperature to 250 -350 ◦ C, the samples become almost insoluble in DMF, suggesting the formation of a highly crosslinked structure. To further characterize the degree of crosslinking, the gel fraction measurement was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the mass  loss  rates  of  cross-linked  membranes  with  temperatures  above 200 ◦ C and time longer than 8 h were below 5%, suggesting a high degree of crosslinking. texts.orig: 3.3. XRD and SEM of PIM crosslinked membranes
texts.text: 3.3. XRD and SEM of PIM crosslinked membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To further explore the effects of crosslinking on membrane structure, XRD analysis was performed to characterize the polymer chain spacing of  membranes. As  shown in Fig.  4,  all  membrane  samples  remained amorphous since no crystalline phase is found. In particular, PIM-COOH and monoesterified PIM membranes exhibit broad peaks which shift to higher  2 θ values,  comparing  with  PIM-1.  It  is  hypothesized  that  the addition  of  carboxyl  and  ester  moieties  of  PIM-COOH  and  M PIM membranes affect the chain spacing, thus reducing the d-spacing, as evidenced by the increased 2 θ values. Moreover, the XRD spectra of membranes exhibit  a  distinct  dual-modal  distribution,  indicating  the existence of two different d-spacings within the membrane, consistent with results reported in literatures [18,20]. On the contrary, compared with untreated PIM, thermally treated membrane samples with different time and temperatures clearly exhibit distinct peaks shifting towards lower  2 θ values,  suggesting  a  higher  d-spacing  in  the  crosslinked membranes, consistent with trends reported in reference [40]. It is expected that the high polymer chain d-spacing obtained in crosslinked membranes results in an enhanced gas permeability of membranes [41], as will be discussed a later section.

texts.orig: The cross-sectional morphology of the ester crosslinked membranes was explored by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 5, SEM results confirms the internal microstructure of PIM membranes,  regardless  of  the  treatment  conditions.  Moreover,  the  crosssections  of  membranes  remain  homogenous  without  any  macro-void defects,  suggesting  the  membranes  are  physically  robust  and  can tolerate the aggressive crosslinking process without damaging the inner structure of membranes. Such a smooth and microporous morphology is essentially  beneficial  for  allowing  fast  gas  transport  in  membranes, which will be discussed in Section 3.5.

texts.orig: thermal stability. For 200 ◦ C treated membranes, the TGA weight loss at ~ 300 ◦ C  generally  decreases  with  the  increase  of  crosslinking  time (Fig. 6 a). Similarly, for membranes thermally treated at 150 -350 ◦ C for 8 h, the higher crosslinking temperature, the low weigh loss at ~ 300 ◦ C. In fact, when the crosslinking temperature is above 250 ◦ C, the weight loss at ~ 300 ◦ C disappears (Fig. 6 d). The decreased initial weight loss of crosslinked membranes at ~ 300 ◦ C is likely attributed to the increase in the degree of crosslinking, which reduces the number of hydroxyl or carboxylic  groups  available  for  crosslinking  reaction  [42 -44].  Such trend  is  consistent  with  the  DMF  dissolution  test  described  above (Fig.  S7 and Fig. S8). These results demonstrate that the ester crosslinking  reaction  improves  the  thermal  stability  of  membrane  significantly [45].

texts.orig: The gas separation permeability and ideal selectivity of PIM-1, PIMCOOH, monoesterified PIM and crosslinked membranes were measured using N2, O2, CH4 and CO2 and summarized in Table 1. All membranes were soaked in methanol and vacuum dried prior to testing. Surprisingly,  the  thermally  crosslinked  membranes  exhibit  simultaneously enhanced gas permeabilities and selectivities,  comparing  with  PIM-1 membranes. For instance, the crosslinked membrane at 300 ◦ C 8 h increases  dramatically  from  4772  Barrer  (PIM-1)  to  7551  Barrers  with enhanced selectivity for CO2/CH4 (more than 11%) and CO2/N2 (more than 20%) compared to PIM-1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To demonstrate the effects of crosslinking conditions on gas separation performance, the gas permeation data are plotted as illustrated in Fig. 7, Fig. 8 and Table 1. As Fig. 7 shows, despite the enhancement of gas  selectivity,  the  hydrolyzed  PIM-COOH  demonstrate  a  significant reduction  of  CO2  permeability  over  84%,  from  4772  to  765  Barrer, comparing  with  PIM-1.  When  further  esterifying  the  PIM-COOH  to prepare M PIM, the CO2  permeability is improved over 5 times and maintains  a  CO2/CH4 and  CO2/N2 of  12.8  and  19.2,  respectively, comparing with PIM-COOH. Fig. 7 also shows that the crosslinking time affects the gas transport in membranes. For membranes crosslinked at 200 ◦ C, the CO2  permeability is apparently higher than uncrosslinked M PIM within 8 h of crosslinking time. This is probably attributed to the micropores  generated  within  the  membrane  by  the  volatilization  of small  by-product  molecules  (i.e.  water)  generated  during  the  ester crosslinking  process.  Such a  microporous  structure  in  membrane  improves  the  gas  permeability,  consistent  with  the  finding  reported  by Koros group in reference [46]. However, when the crosslinking time exceeds 8 h (i.e. 12 h or 24 h in this work), a reduced of CO2  permeability emerges, possibly attributed to the accelerated aging of membrane  samples  during  extended  thermal  annealing.  Therefore,  we judiciously selected 8 h as the optimal crosslinking time for further study of effects of crosslinking temperatures on membrane performance.

texts.orig: As Fig. 8 depicts, the crosslinking temperature plays a key role in regulating  gas  separation  properties  of  membranes.  Compared  with uncrosslinked  M PIM,  the  crosslinked  membranes  display  improved CO2 permeability. In particular, under a thermal treatment temperature below 300 ◦ C, the higher the crosslinking temperature, the higher CO2 permeability of membranes. However, when the crosslinking temperature rises to 350 ◦ C, a loss of CO2 permeability occurs, likely owing to the partial decomposition of polymers as also revealed in the TGA analysis in Fig. 6 a. On the other hand, the crosslinked membranes show higher CO2/CH4  selectivities than both M PIM and PIM-1. For instance, the CO2/CH4  selectivity  of  200 ◦ C-8  h  crosslinked  sample  reaches  16.9, enhanced  by  over  80%,  compared  with  PIM-1  ( α CO2/CH4 = 9.3).  In addition, the CO2/N2  selectivity of crosslinked membranes are comparable  with  M PIM  in  most  cases  and  much  higher  than  PIM-1 membranes.

texts.orig: To  further  illustrate  the  gas  separation  performance,  the  gas permeation results are plotted and compared with upper bounds (Fig. 9). The relevant literature data can obtain form Table S1 and Ref. [31 34 4 -55].  As  one  can  see,  PIM-1,  PIM-COOH,  and  monoesterified  PIM membranes exhibit moderate separation performance below the Robeson upper bound [9]. Upon ester-crosslinking, the membranes demonstrate improved separation properties, performing beyond the Robeson upper bounds and close to the latest upper bounds for both CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 [8]. Those results suggest that ester-crosslinking is an effective approach to modulate the gas transport properties of PIM membranes, enabling the formation of membranes with enhanced permeability and selectivity suited for CO2 separation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Plasticization resistance of crosslinked membranes
texts.text: 3.6. Plasticization resistance of crosslinked membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To examine the CO2 plasticization behavior, crosslinked membranes under different temperatures and times were measured with pure CO2 at feed  pressures  up  to  42  bar  and  results  are  depicted  in  Fig.  10.  As illustrated in Fig. 10 a, the uncrosslinked PIM-COOH and monoesterified PIM membranes exhibit sharp increases in CO2 permeability when the test pressure exceeds 6.9 bar, indicative of the emergence of plasticization. Moreover, the 200 ◦ C 2 h treated membranes also shows plasticization behavior, consistent with the DMF dissolution results where they are insoluble in the first 2 h but become soluble after 24 h. However, membranes crosslinked over 8 h demonstrate strong plasticization resistance as the permeability does not increase with pressures as high as 42 bar. Instead, a slight drop of permeability was found, likely due to the compressing  effect  of  high  pressure  on  PIM  membranes,  which  are essentially semi-rigid [56]. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 10 b, membranes with thermal treatment temperatures of 200 -350 ◦ C for 8 h do not show upswing of permeability up to 42 bar, indicative of crosslinking achieved in membrane samples. It is believed that the ester crosslinking establishes  an  interconnected  structure  of  polymer  network  in  the membranes, which reduces the polymer chain mobility and increases the CO2  plasticization resistance [35]. The resistance to CO2  plasticization obtained in the crosslinked PIM membranes provides potentials for applications with high CO2 pressure feed streams, including natural gas processing.
